sentences.text: It was in February, towards the end of February in 1943.
And--
Beg your pardon?
I was in Augsburg at Hans Dieter's camp.
And there was a bombardment.
We run to the shelter.
The shelter was outside of our fence.
And we were always driven there, taken there, by the guards.
Because they-- I believe the factory wanted their people to be safe.
And the bomb had struck near the-- at the other end of the bunker.
And there was panic, and panic ensued.
Everybody was running everywhere, to all sides.
Even the guards they didn't watch-- watch us anymore.
So I started running.
And I was running, running into the fields.
There were fields nearby because we were living-- the factory was at the outskirts of the-- of the town.
And I hid, finally under the bushes and like a scared rabbit, thinking, what next?
And somebody-- on the other side of the bushes, somebody crawled in as well.
And I thought it was some animal or something.
And then-- and then it was a-- a friend from-- from the camp who decided to call my name.
She recognized me.
And we were thinking both aloud, what are we going to do?
So we decided to run off.
And we didn't know which way or where because a map was not in our-- our fortune.
So we just went into the fields.
And we were-- we've noticed there-- there was a highway.
And we went on that highway.
And we were going-- marching all through the night.
And looking sometimes to the-- to the-- back towards the town, which was burning.
You could see the flames here and there.
And finally afterwards, maybe one day, it was almost dawning, I came-- we came to this field where somebody was coming with a wagon and taking manure into the field.
And we heard this somebody singing in Ukrainian.
So we, of coarse, Ukrainian, or Polish, or-- or whatever, that was a sign of our saving ourselves.
We ran to him and, of course, he spoke also Polish.
And we were talking to him and explaining to him what happened.
And he-- and he said, well, I'll take you to my boss and maybe he will find some work for you two.
And that's the way I was-- I was employed by one lady in this town, this village, rather.
There were all of about 10 farming-- farming people-- farms-- 10 farms in one group, not too many.
That was outside of Gablinger.
And this lady, her-- her husband was on the Russian front.
She was alone with her mother and her son, little son.
And I stayed with them for a while.
I believe that her name is Sophie.
And I don't remember whether [?
Schnitzler ?] or something like that.
Schnitzler, ?] I believe.
And her grandmother was Clara.
And the boy was Vince.
And--
My friend was taken by another farmer.
There were two men.
They had no woman in their home.
So she was employed as a domestic and as a housekeeper.
It was not safe.
I believe they-- what we told them that our camp was bombed and we have nowhere to go.
So they didn't analyze too much that.
And they heard about the bombing of Augsburg.
So they stayed quiet.
And since my friend, for example, was very useful to the-- to the men, they were very helpful to her.
And apparently, they were even kind to her, which was a surprise to me.
And as to my women, they used me as a hard worker, a domestic.
I did the hard-- heavier tasks.
And one day I was asked to carry-- that was already in beginning of April-- carry the grain from the attic.
They had big barrels of grain in the attic.
And we were putting them in the bags.
And I was going to carry them to the-- to the truck, to the wagon.
And somehow or other, I tripped on the stairs.
And I fell down with that-- with this heavy load on my back.
And I hurt my-- my back.
I hurt my back to the point that I got paralyzed, couldn't move.
So the women were quite scared, quite uneasy.
And I was scared as well that they don't call the authorities.
Because if they would, they would take me to hospital and from there back to Dachau and you know where to.
And I was begging, especially the grand-- grandma, Clara, to keep me for awhile, just trying to delay it and not do anything.
And she was massaging my back.
Massaging it, she really brought me to-- to previous condition that I could walk.
And slowly, I started walking and working around in the kitchen a little bit, helping, peeling potatoes, cleaning a little bit here and there while they were going to the fields.
And Frau Sophie was not too happy about that.
I believe she must have notified the authorities.
And the day when they came for me somehow, I believe, they didn't want to witness their betraying me.
So they left for the fields.
And the gestapo came.
And they took me in the-- this-- they came on a motorcycle, you know, in the car, a sidecar in the motorcycle, you know the sidecar.
And they were driving me, which is a very strange thing.
I-- to this day, I don't know what happened.
They were riding with me to a forest.
I noticed now instead of going the regular highway to-- leading to Augsburg.
They were taking me to the forest.
So I was sure they were going to take advantage of me.
They were going to shoot me, or destroy me anyway.
I was prepared already for my last day.
And as they came into the forest, they tied me down to the tree.
And they started all kinds of silly jumping around and thumping and showing off and diving, showing off, taking off their pants and so on.
And at a certain moment some old-- some guy, some old probably farmer-- I don't know where he came from and what he was for-- came to that.
And seeing it, he called him.
And they looked around to him.
And I don't know what happened.
They were so fast.
I [INAUDIBLE].
And they were so fast in untying me and putting me back into that-- that motorcycle.
And they drove me at full speed to Augsburg.
And I believe that was-- if you believe in angels-- that was an angel.
I don't think there was a-- and when I came there, and I was praying so much, thanking God that I came fine and safe out a it, was so relieved.
Oh my God, I know it doesn't matter maybe.
I will probably be punished in some small way.
But that time, at least, I know where I am put and away from those henchmen.
And they took me to the office.
And as if scared, they left at once, the two.
And some other two men came, also guards.
And they took me to the inside of the magazine where they are holding all the stuff for exploding the-- the [INAUDIBLE] or tarps.
And they took me to one room, which was empty, except that it was full of whips and things there.
It was probably a punishment room.
And they started beating me.
They beat me so hard that, finally, I lost consciousness.
When I came to, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I didn't know where I was.
I opened my eyes, but I couldn't see anything.
And I didn't believe to myself.
Am I blind?
Or what is it?
Where am I?
So I started groping around.
And I noticed that this was a dirt soil around me.
And I wanted to see how high is that soil and where am I.
And so suddenly I realized I must be buried alive.
And so I tried to lift myself up.
And it was too hard because I couldn't.
I tried, and I couldn't even say my-- I mean talk.
I was opening my mouth.
I was trying to call for help, but nothing was coming out.
And when I tried to lift myself up, I fell down back.
I fell back down.
And I fainted again.
And I don't know how long I was there.
When I woke up I heard human voices.
So I believe-- I don't know what gave me the strength to call.
And I was calling, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
And somebody lifted-- there was this, you know, like a-- this heavy round thing above.
And they lifted it.
And they looked inside.
And they were asking, what you're doing there, rat?
And I couldn't explain that I was only repeating the same thing.
Because I didn't know much of German language.
And I couldn't-- I could hardly speak.
I don't know how I really made myself understood.
So they went somewhere.
And I thought, oh, God, they will probably leave me alone.
And they-- after awhile they came with a rope, with a noose around it.
They-- and they brought me up.
They asked me to get my legs in there.
And they pulled me up.
And they took me to the latrines, where I sat for a while.
They left all at once.
And I didn't-- I never seen them anymore in my life.
And they left me there.
I washed myself a little after a while.
And of course, there are no mirrors, nothing to even see what-- in what condition I was.
And I was wondering how and where am I?
And where am I going to go now?
And there was a-- this charwomen who came after a while.
And she started shrieking in her German way.
And holding her head like that and asking, what's the matter?
What's happened?
And this [? one. ?] And I said, take me.
And she asked me, where-- where are you coming from?
Where are you from?
And I say, I said, down-- downstairs, downstairs where the women are.
And she said, well, I'll take you downstairs.
But don't tell anybody I took you.
So she took me downstairs.
And she even put me in one of the empty-- the nearby beds.
And that's how the women, when they came back from work, they found me there, lying there.
And the rest is drudgery, recovery.
And of course, I lost my teeth, which I never really replaced.
The-- the person that was in the infirmary pulled the teeth out without any anesthetic or anything.
Because they were hanging there anyway.
And they took me after three days.
They asked me to go back to work.
I was there till '45.
It was the end of February.
And at that time, earlier than that, I came to know this lady who came, I believe, in either June or August to the-- to the-- to that place.
This lady came.
I have a picture of her here.
She came to hold the barrack.
She was sent by some farmers to our camp.
And we were talking and became really, really, very great friends.
She was a Polish aristocrat, a very sentimental, very well quite learned.
Yelena.
And she was discussing a lot of things with me.
And-- and whenever I got hold of any paper, for example, any toilet paper, I wrote little poems about-- even there I wrote poems.
And about-- I wrote a song, for example, for Christmas, which later on, the women were singing even.
And we were very great friends.
And she said, well, since the rumors were going around that people who incurred the wrath of the-- of the Germans, they're going to go back to Dachau.
And therefore, and they're going to be gassed and cremated there.
So the fear, terrible fear, panic, almost, started, knowing in my guts.
And I said to myself, I have to run away again.
I can't stand that.
I cannot go to-- back to Dachau.
And that's the way we were planning and going through it and waiting for eventually.
There was a blackout.
And while there was a blackout, I-- for quite a few weeks, I have noticed that there was one window that was not entirely closed in the latrines.
And I could get outside while there was the bombardment again.
And I-- I took the advantage of that.
My friend Yelena gave me her coat that she was given by her friend, by her baron or farmer lady.
She gave me that coat.
And she gave me the ticket.
She asked me to go to the place where she was, to her-- to her baron.
Baron is a farmer lady.
OK?
And I managed to escape that night when there was a blackout.
And there was it that you went to?
And I went to [INAUDIBLE].
[INAUDIBLE], I went.
I remember I arrived there at night by train after many other adventures.
And I was going to that forest.
There was a forest between the station.
Between the train station and the village there was a forest-- it probably still is-- where you go for about, I don't know, half an hour or so.
And it was night.
And I was so afraid.
Because I didn't know who I might encounter and what-- where am I really going.
Because she described everything to me.
But what is written and what is said and what is drawn is one thing.
But another thing is to experience it.
And then when finally I got there, I went to these people.
And they were somewhat annoyed by my intrusion.
And-- but worked this way.
And they sent me to this lady who needed somebody really badly to work for her.
And I worked for this lady.
Her name was-- what was her name?
Gertrude, or-- Gertrude.
Yeah.
And she was-- she had a Serbian guy who also worked for her.
And the Serbian guy was very nice man about 45 or so.
And he also said, you would-- you would be better if you stay in the fields.
Don't show yourself.
So they both decided that I should be staying in the fields most of the time.
I only came home at night under the cover of the night.
And I worked in the-- in her fields.
And at night, I was coming and staying.
And she had a little room upstairs, like in the attic.
And she had two little girls.
And-- Ania, what was the discussion with the people you stayed with, either there or in Gablinger, in the farm?
What was-- what did they know about what was going on in the war?
In the camps, specifically?
These people-- these people were somehow negating it.
These people could not believe it.
They were saying, the German Reich, or nation, needs people to work.
Since we are the-- we have acquired this part of the-- of the country, we have the rights to employ some of the people for the time being.
After the war you are going to go back home.
Everything will be fine.
Well, I told them but not everything.
Because I just didn't want to sort of aggravate that person.
She might not believe me.
And then she might be hostile to me and might even tell me move out.
No, no they didn't-- certainly that's the one thing that they would never mention and never-- I don't know whether-- I don't think they knew about it.
At least the people with whom I was, they were very down to earth peasants, working their farms, and cleaning their houses, and being-- being quite cozy in this situation.
I believe that whoever did that were mostly people who worked very hard at destroying people.
And their kapos, or henchmen, were taken out from mostly from the prisons so that they could atone for whatever they did.
And they were just exercising their power.
And it was-- it was simply, to me, I believe, of course the whole nation has been sort of brainwashed by these politicians.
In May 1945, I was already-- well, on May 20-- 17, the American Army came in.
I was in the field.
I remember.
And I was running like crazy home.
Because I started-- I heard the staccato of the machine guns.
And I-- I ran to the-- to her place.
And she wasn't there anymore.
And I had my-- even my lunch and everything.
And towards the evening, there was-- there was Army unit came on her yard, quite a lot of military, quite a lot of soldiers around.
And they came into the house.
And I have to tell you that, because this is very important.
I was alone in the house.
I didn't know where the woman was, nor the girls.
And where the Boris-- Boris went, I don't  know.
Maybe he hid too.
Because to me, he was a-- he was a traitor, collaborator to me.
Because he came voluntarily to work.
Yes.
Anyway, he was a-- he was a good man.
I should not other-- in any other way.
Towards me he was all right.
And I was going-- I went upstairs to my-- to sleep.
And then I hear the commotion downstairs.
So I come in, come in-- I am coming down on the stairs.
And there-- there-- there's two soldiers coming up the stairs towards me.
And for some reason, one grabs me.
And the other is shouting something, of course, in English.
I didn't understand English at that time.
And he grabs by the hand.
And I, seeing that, I just had this strange moment of escape, the final, you know-- it's just instinctively.
I jumped over the rails, jumped down.
And somehow he let-- somehow I slipped out of his hand.
I hurt a little bit my-- my tushy.
And [LAUGHS] I-- I run out.
There was a-- there was this wash room outside, the outhouse.
Yes.
